hints.febri and illam: 'febri' is a noun (fever) and 'illam' is a pronoun (her/it)
hints.imperavit only: There are two finite verbs joined by 'et' — both 'imperavit' and 'dimisit'
hints.dimisit only: There are two finite verbs joined by 'et' — both 'imperavit' and 'dimisit'

hints.to rule: 'to rule' is 'regnare/imperare' can mean this, but the primary meaning is 'to command'

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'imperat' — 'imperavit' with -avit is perfect

hints.impedio, impedire, impedivi, impeditum: These are principal parts of 'impedio' (to hinder)
hints.paro, parare, paravi, paratum: These are principal parts of 'paro' (to prepare)
hints.rego, regere, rexi, rectum: These are principal parts of 'rego' (to rule/direct)

hints.to destroy: 'to destroy' is 'delere' — 'dimisit' comes from 'dimittere' (to send away)
hints.to dismiss a case: 'dimittere' means 'to send away/release', not a legal term specifically

hints.mitto, mittere, misi, missum: These are principal parts of 'mitto' (to send) — close, but 'dimitto' is the compound
hints.dico, dicere, dixi, dictum: These are principal parts of 'dico' (to say)
hints.divido, dividere, divisi, divisum: These are principal parts of 'divido' (to divide)

hints.nominative: Nominative of 'febris' is 'febris' — 'febri' is dative (he commanded the fever)
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'febrim' — 'febri' is dative, governed by 'imperavit'
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'febri' (same form), but 'imperare' takes the dative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension nouns have genitive in -ae — 'febris' has genitive in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension nouns have genitive in -i — 'febris' has genitive in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension nouns have genitive in -us — 'febris' has genitive in -is

translation: He commanded the fever and it left her
